sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with right elbow pain.
He reports experiencing pain in the outside part of his elbow for the last three weeks, with a severity of 8/10 when using the arm and 3/10 when resting.
The pain is described as sharp, stabbing, and aching, and is worse with movement and better with rest.
He denies any recent trauma or pop.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide relief.
He also reports trying ice, but it did not help.
He has a history of playing tennis, which he has increased recently.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, mild intermittent asthma, and a surgical history of gallbladder removal 10 years ago.
He has an allergy to penicillin.
He is currently taking metformin 500mg twice a day and flovent twice a day, and albuterol as needed.
Review of systems: The patient denies fever.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Flovent twice a day for asthma
Albuterol as needed for asthma
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's heart exam is normal with a grade 3 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur.
His musculoskeletal exam reveals moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion and resisted extension of the wrist.
There is no palpable joint effusion.
Test Results:
The patient's right elbow x-ray shows no acute fracture or other bony abnormality, with no malalignment or sign of joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse from increased tennis activity.
Problem list: Tennis elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50mg once a day for pain relief.
I recommended that the patient rest his right arm as much as possible, stop playing tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress in the right arm.
I also recommended that the patient ice the right elbow for 20 minutes, three times a day.
I wrote a prescription for a brace for the right forearm to help with discomfort.
I increased the patient's metformin to 1000mg twice a day for diabetes management.
I ordered a hemoglobin A1c level, a CBC blood test, and a chem-12 blood test.
I also wrote a prescription for a refill of albuterol for asthma.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or a physical therapy referral if needed.
